data.source: Laboratory experiments with undergraduate students at Arizona State University involving token investments and resource extraction in a simulated bandwidth/irrigation setting
data.wave_or_subset: 2-person and 5-person group treatments; additional treatment with interruptions where incomplete downloads could be lost due to upstream blockages; communication allowed between rounds
data.unit_of_analysis: Individual participant decisions within groups across repeated rounds (participants nested in groups; group-level analyses also conducted)
data.notes: Asymmetric access implemented via spatial position determining extraction order (upstream/downstream). Each round included two phases: invest in public infrastructure (bandwidth) via a sigmoidal production function, then appropriate/download with priority access. Repeated-interaction data with chat logs; hierarchical (participants within groups, repeated rounds).

method.description: The study used controlled laboratory experiments to simulate asymmetric commons dilemmas akin to irrigation systems. Participants, arranged in 2- or 5-person groups with ordered access (upstream to downstream), repeatedly decided how many tokens to invest in shared infrastructure (bandwidth) and then attempted to extract benefits by downloading files, where upstream players had priority. Treatments included communication and an interruption condition where incomplete downloads could be lost. The production of bandwidth followed a sigmoidal function of joint investments. Data on investments, downloads, earnings, and chat messages were collected across rounds. Analyses included derivation of Nash and cooperative equilibria, and hierarchical linear modeling to assess determinants of investment and earnings while accounting for group-level structure.
steps: Recruit undergraduate subjects and assign them to 2-person or 5-person groups with fixed positional order (upstream to downstream).
Initialize each round with an endowment of 10 tokens per participant (each token worth $0.10).
Phase 1 (Provision): Participants decide how many tokens to invest in public infrastructure; joint investment determines available bandwidth via a sigmoidal production function displayed to subjects.
Phase 2 (Appropriation): Participants download files using the generated bandwidth in positional order, giving upstream players priority; measure number of downloads and resulting earnings.
Implement experimental treatments: (a) group size (2 vs. 5), (b) communication allowed between rounds, and (c) interruption condition where incomplete downloads can be lost due to upstream blockages.
Repeat the provision and appropriation phases over multiple (10) rounds, recording investments, downloads, earnings, and chat messages each round.
Compute descriptive statistics (investments, downloads, earnings) and inequality measures (e.g., Gini).
Derive theoretical Nash and cooperative equilibria for comparison with observed behavior.

Estimate hierarchical linear models/regressions to assess how position, round, and prior extraction shares influence investment and earnings; include group-level predictors (e.g., share of economics majors) and account for within-group variability.
method.models: Hierarchical linear model (multilevel) for investment behavior; regression analyses relating tokens earned to bandwidth; theoretical derivation of Nash and cooperative equilibria
method.outcome_variable: Tokens earned from downloading; investment in public infrastructure (tokens invested); number of files downloaded; inequality of earnings (Gini coefficient)
method.independent_variables: Player position (upstream/downstream), round number, prior-round share of tokens earned from extraction, available bandwidth, treatment indicators (communication, interruptions), group size (2 vs. 5), group composition (share of economics majors), inequality of earnings
method.control_variables: Group-level effects to account for within-group variability; treatment dummies; time/round trends

results.summary: Participants frequently invested above the Nash prediction but often failed to reach the cooperative optimum due to coordination challenges, especially in 5-person groups. Upstream (head-end) participants appropriated more, leading to significant disparities in downloads and earnings relative to downstream participants. Downstream participants sometimes reduced investments in response to perceived unfair appropriation, indirectly punishing upstream players and decreasing overall infrastructure provision. Communication improved cooperative investments and coordination over time, with inequality (initially high, Gini ~0.4–0.45) decreasing across rounds. Hierarchical models indicated that position, round, and prior extraction shares influenced investment; more economics majors in a group correlated with lower investments. Increased bandwidth was associated with higher tokens earned, but downstream positions earned fewer tokens and required higher investments to offset their disadvantage.

numerical_results.outcome_name: Initial inequality of earnings (Gini coefficient, lower bound of reported range)
